content.text: Platform friction tracking is the practice of continuously collecting, categorizing, and scoring signals that indicate where developers are losing time or getting blocked on an internal platform. Combined with a disciplined backlog prioritization process, it ensures that the platform team's sprint capacity goes toward the highest-impact pain points rather than whoever filed the most recent complaint.
What it is

Friction tracking is a structured system for turning unstructured developer signals — support tickets, Slack messages, survey responses, CLI error telemetry, and TTFAD stage deltas — into a ranked backlog of platform improvements. Each friction item is assigned a score based on three factors: how often the friction occurs (frequency), how much engineering time it costs per occurrence (severity), and how many distinct teams are affected (reach).

The output is a friction register: a living document or database table that lists every known platform pain point ordered by its composite friction score. The platform team reviews this register at the start of each sprint and uses it as the primary input to backlog refinement. A friction item that falls below a severity threshold is closed as "won't fix" or deferred to a future quarter.
Why it matters

Without friction tracking, backlog prioritization defaults to whoever shouts loudest or whoever has the most organisational clout. A developer from a high-visibility team files a ticket about a minor aesthetic issue in the portal; it gets fixed in one sprint. Meanwhile, a recurring CI secrets rotation error that blocks 40% of new deployments persists for a quarter because it only surfaces as individual Slack questions, never as a unified ticket.

Friction tracking makes the invisible visible. It aggregates distributed signals into a single ranked list, making it impossible for the platform team to inadvertently prioritize novelty over impact. It also builds developer trust: when engineers can see that their pain is captured in a public register and being worked on in priority order, they stop routing around the platform and start engaging with the feedback loop.
Benefits
Evidence-based prioritization: every backlog item links back to quantified signals rather than anecdote, making priority decisions defensible to stakeholders.
Deduplication: aggregating signals reveals that what looks like twelve separate tickets is actually one underlying infrastructure issue — reducing resolution effort dramatically.
Developer trust: a public friction register with status updates signals that the platform team is listening and acting, increasing voluntary engagement with the feedback loop.
Capacity protection: a scored register gives the platform team objective grounds to say no to low-impact requests that arrive with high organizational urgency.
Trend detection: comparing register snapshots week-over-week reveals whether friction is increasing (platform regressing) or decreasing (platform improving).
Escalation justification: a friction score aggregating large amounts of developer time lost is a compelling input to engineering leadership budget discussions.
Trade-offs and limitations
Scoring model subjectivity: the severity weights (how many hours a given error type costs) require calibration through developer interviews — wrong weights produce misleading rankings.
Signal coverage gaps: friction that developers work around silently (they found a workaround and never filed a ticket) will not appear in the register until telemetry or TTFAD catches the symptom.
Maintenance overhead: the register must be actively curated. Stale items that have been resolved but not closed distort the priority ordering and erode trust.

Gaming risk: if the register becomes a vanity metric, teams may close friction items by documenting workarounds rather than fixing root causes, driving score down without improving developer experience.

Lagging for novel issues: the scoring model rewards recurring issues over novel ones. A new integration that is breaking for a small number of teams may not score highly until it has accumulated enough incidents.
How it works

Signals arrive from multiple sources simultaneously. A Kafka consumer subscribes to the platform event stream and the CLI error telemetry topic, tagging each error event with a component label (CI, scaffold, deploy, portal, SDK). A separate Slack bot monitors the #help-platform channel and extracts thread starts with developer-reported friction keywords (stuck, broken, failing, timeout, permission). Support tickets are polled from Jira or Linear via API and categorized by component label.

The aggregator clusters similar signals using simple keyword matching plus a component tag: two Slack threads and three tickets all mentioning 'secrets rotation' and tagged 'CI' are merged into a single friction item. The friction score for that item is computed as: (incidents_per_week) x (median_engineer_hours_per_occurrence) x (unique_teams_reporting). This is recalculated every Monday morning and the register is sorted descending by score.
Severity calibration

Severity (the engineer-hours-per-occurrence weight) is the hardest factor to measure. The best approach is to run a quarterly calibration survey: show developers a list of 10 common friction scenarios and ask them to estimate how long each took to resolve. Aggregate the median estimates and use them as severity weights in the formula. Update the weights annually or after major platform changes.
Closing the loop

A friction item is closed when: (a) a fix ships to production, (b) the friction score drops below threshold in the two weeks after the fix, and (c) a changelog entry is posted to the #platform-updates Slack channel referencing the friction item ID. This three-step close prevents premature closure and creates an audit trail that developers can reference to confirm their pain was addressed.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Platform friction tracking flow: signal sources to aggregator to friction score to prioritized backlog, with closed-loop resolution

content.text: Signal sources feed into a friction aggregator that tags, deduplicates, and clusters pain points. The aggregator computes a friction score (frequency x severity x reach) and outputs a ranked friction register. The platform team translates high-scoring register items into sprint backlog tickets. After each fix ships, the loop closes: a changelog entry is posted, the friction score is verified to decline, and a digest of resolved frictions is published to developers.
When to use it
Sprint planning: use the friction register as the primary input to backlog refinement, targeting the top 3-5 items each sprint.
Quarterly roadmap planning: items that have been on the register for more than two quarters without resolution are candidates for a dedicated work track rather than sprint slice.

Post-incident review: after any platform outage or regression, check whether the root cause already appeared in the friction register. If it did and was deprioritized, update the scoring model to weight that signal type more heavily.
OKR setting: use the aggregate friction score across all register items as a platform health KPI to improve by a target percentage each quarter.
How to implement
Enumerate your signal sources. At minimum: support tickets, a designated Slack channel, and CLI error telemetry. Add TTFAD stage deltas and DevEx survey responses as you mature.

Build a simple aggregator: a weekly cron job that queries all sources, applies keyword-based component tagging, and clusters similar items. Store results in a friction register table in your data warehouse.
Define your friction score formula and calibrate severity weights through a developer interview or estimation survey before rolling out the first register.
Publish the friction register as a Notion page, Confluence page, or internal dashboard. Include score, component, signal count, and status. Update weekly.
Add a register review to your sprint planning ceremony: the team votes on which top-N items to pull into the upcoming sprint, using the score as the default ordering.
Establish a close protocol: a friction item is only closed when the fix is shipped, the score verifiably declines, and a changelog entry is published.
Track register health metrics: number of open items, average score trend, mean time from item creation to close. Review quarterly.
Examples

-- Friction register query: top 20 open items this week
SELECT
  friction_id,
  component,
  description,
  incident_count,
  unique_team_count,
  ROUND(incident_count * severity_weight * unique_team_count, 1) AS friction_score,
  first_seen_date,
  DATEDIFF('day', first_seen_date, CURRENT_DATE) AS age_days,
  status
FROM friction_register
WHERE status = 'OPEN'
  AND week = DATE_TRUNC('week', CURRENT_DATE)
ORDER BY friction_score DESC
LIMIT 20;
Real-world example

A media technology company's AI platform team was drowning in a backlog of 60+ open tickets with no clear priority order. They implemented friction tracking using a weekly Python script that queried Jira, the #help-ai-platform Slack channel (via Slack API), and their OpenTelemetry error events table in BigQuery. The aggregator ran on Monday mornings and posted the top-10 friction register to a Confluence page.

In the first week, the top-scoring item (score 42) was 'CUDA out of memory during model loading in staging GPU pods' — an issue that had been filed by 8 different teams as separate tickets and had collectively cost an estimated 160 developer-hours that quarter. It had never appeared as a single consolidated issue. The team dedicated the next sprint to it, shipped a fix, and watch the aggregate friction score drop by 35% in two weeks. Developer NPS improved 12 points the following quarter.
Common mistakes
Building the register but not publishing it: a private register that only the platform team sees does not build developer trust or reduce the volume of duplicated tickets.
Scoring without calibrated severity weights: using a uniform severity weight of 1.0 for all components treats a 30-second portal confusion the same as a 4-hour CI failure.
Closing items prematurely: closing a ticket when the fix is merged rather than when the score verifiably declines allows regressions to slip through unnoticed.
Not deduplicating: each signal treated as a separate item creates a bloated register that obscures the true priority ordering.
Ignoring silent friction: friction that developers work around without filing tickets is systematically undercounted. Supplement with TTFAD stage-delta spikes to catch silent issues.
Best practices
Run the aggregator on a fixed weekly cadence so the register is always fresh for Monday sprint planning.
Limit the actionable register to the top 20 items; longer lists cause analysis paralysis in sprint planning.
Assign a DRI (directly responsible individual) to each open register item — unowned items do not get resolved.
Publish weekly delta: highlight which items entered the register, which rose in score, and which were closed. This shows developers the system is alive.
Review the scoring formula quarterly and recalibrate severity weights based on recent developer interviews.
Performance and scalability

The aggregator itself is cheap to run: even with thousands of signals per week, a weekly batch job completes in under a minute. The main cost is ongoing curation of the register, which requires a dedicated person (often the platform team's engineering manager or a rotating DRI) spending 30-60 minutes per week on triage.

As the platform grows and signal volume increases, invest in better clustering: move from keyword matching to embedding-based similarity clustering (e.g., using a small sentence transformer to cluster similar issue descriptions). This reduces false-negative deduplication (two Slack messages about the same issue that use different vocabulary are no longer missed).
Tooling and ecosystem
Tool
Role
Notes
Jira / Linear API
Source: support tickets
Use component labels for automatic tagging
Slack API
Source: #help-platform messages
Monitor thread starts for friction keywords
OpenTelemetry / BigQuery
Source: CLI and platform error telemetry
Tag events with component and error_type
Python / dbt
Aggregation and scoring pipeline
Weekly cron; store results in a data warehouse table
Notion / Confluence
Publish friction register
Embed live table from data warehouse via API
PagerDuty / Grafana
Alert on friction score spikes
Alert when any component score rises >50% WoW
Comparison with alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Weaknesses
Friction register (scored)
Quantified, deduped, ranked
Requires setup effort and ongoing curation
Ad-hoc ticket triage
No tooling required
Prioritizes recency over impact; opaque to developers
Developer NPS survey
Direct satisfaction signal
Quarterly cadence; no component-level breakdown
On-call retrospectives
Rich context from incidents
Only captures outage-level issues; misses sub-critical friction
TTFAD alone
Objective behavioral signal
Does not capture friction outside the onboarding journey
Security and reliability

The friction register contains descriptions of platform weaknesses. Access should be internal-only but broadly readable within engineering — restricting it to the platform team removes the trust-building benefit. Do not include PII (developer names) in the public register; use pseudonymous identifiers or aggregate team-level attribution only.

For reliability of the aggregator pipeline: if the weekly job fails to run, the register becomes stale and sprint planning will use outdated data. Add a freshness check: alert the platform team lead if the register has not been updated in more than 8 days. Store the previous week's register snapshot so that a failed run does not result in an empty register.
Key takeaways
Platform friction tracking aggregates signals from tickets, Slack, telemetry, and surveys into a ranked friction register updated weekly.
The friction score (frequency x severity x reach) provides an objective basis for backlog prioritization that replaces loudest-voice-wins dynamics.
Severity weights must be calibrated through developer interviews — uniform weights produce misleading rankings.
The register must be public within engineering: visibility is what builds developer trust and increases voluntary feedback loop participation.
A friction item is only closed when the fix ships, the score verifiably declines, and a changelog entry is published — not when the PR merges.
Deduplication is the single highest-leverage step: many scattered signals about the same root cause should resolve to one scored item.
Track register health as a platform OKR: total open score, mean item age, and weekly score delta are useful platform health indicators.
